targets `map-fog`, `map-marker-new`, `route-path`.\n- `routeDrawScene`: production; targets `route-path`.\n- `markerStampScene`: future-contract only; must remain future/`architecture_ready`, not falsely production.\n- `shipCourseScene`: future-contract only.\n- Registry contracts currently require one `map-marker-new` or one `route-path`, default cardinality max `1`.\n\n### Confirmed target and ownership defects\n\n1. Permanent marker same-node conflict:\n   - `VoyageChart` puts `layout`, `data-scene-part=\"map-marker-new\"`, and `data-gsap-owned` on the same `motion.button`.\n   - Motion layout writes transform; GSAP scenes write scale/rotate/opacity.\n   - `.illustrated-marker` also writes `transform: translate(-50%, -50%)`, creating a CSS/Motion transform collision.\n\n2. Broad-host duplicate conflict:\n   - While Chart is active, the permanent chart and `PlayerProgressionProp` both expose `map-marker-new`, `route-path`, and `map-fog` inside the same `<main>` playback root.\n   - Preflight therefore sees duplicate required targets; scene builders’ `parts(context.root, ...)` can select both.\n   - Root DOM says host kind `player-progression`, but nonchapter `director.play()` explicitly passes compatibility kind `progression`; Phase 2 should remove this identity split.\n\n3. “Newest” target is not semantic:\n   - Permanent marker selection uses an array index.\n   - Snapshots order locations by `key`, not reveal chronology.\n   - The index is calculated after filtering coordinates but compared to the unfiltered array length; a coordinate-less rumored location can leave no `map-marker-new`.\n   - Event payloads already provide exact location/route keys, so target identity must use those keys or explicit handles.\n\n4. Every permanent revealed route uses `data-scene-part=\"route-path\"`.\n   - More than one route produces duplicates if the chart itself becomes the scene host.\n   - Only the event-selected route may expose the active scene part; all routes need stable route-key identities.\n\n5. `data-gsap-owned` is not enforcement.\n   - Current ownership code reads `data-animation-owner`, while Phase 2 requires runtime claims with host/instance/claim identity.\n   - Static attributes remain diagnostics only.\n\n6. Reduced-mode scene gap:\n   - `mapRevealScene` still starts the marker at `scale: 2.4` and `rotate: -18`; only duration is shortened.\n   - `markerStampScene` similarly starts at scale `2`.\n   - This conflicts with the no-spatial-travel reduced policy. `map-reveal` needs an immediate/nonspatial reduced pose; `marker-stamp` must not be counted implemented merely because its future contract is retained.\n\n### Recommended wrapper ownership\n\n```tsx\n<motion.button\n  className=\"illustrated-marker-layout\"\n  layout=\"position\"\n  data-animation-owner=\"motion\"\n  aria-label={stableAccessibleName}\n>\n  <span\n    className=\"illustrated-marker-visual\"\n    data-scene-part={isExplicitEventTarget ? \"map-marker-new\" : undefined}\n    data-scene-target-id={`map-marker:${location.key}`}\n    data-animation-owner=\"gsap\"\n    aria-hidden=\"true\"\n  >\n    <span className=\"illustrated-marker-symbol\">✦</span>\n    <b>{location.name}</b>\n    <small>{location.regionLabel}</small>\n  </span>\n</motion.button>\n```\n\n- Outer semantic button: Motion layout/press transform, focus, native activation.\n- Inner visual: GSAP stamp/pulse/bounded reveal.\n- CSS: material, background, shadow, typography, static state only.\n- Move centering off CSS `transform`; express it through the Motion-owned layer or a non-transform positioning wrapper.\n- Refine the broad `.illustrated-marker span` selector after wrapper insertion.\n- Permanent chart should be isolated as `player-section-enhancement`; temporary event overlay should be a distinct `player-progression` SceneHost.\n- Use explicit key-based target handles for chart handoff; never DOM order or “last index.”\n\n### Semantics, focus, and reduced constraints\n\n- Keep the `button` role and accessible name on the Motion outer node.\n- Keep the GSAP visual child hidden from assistive technology; the button’s `aria-label` supplies its name.\n- Preserve the existing accessible `.map-alternative` and `.route-list`; motion cannot be the only state signal.\n- Preserve the global `:focus-visible` ring and focus across rerenders keyed by `location.key`.\n- Arrow-key pan remains on the chart viewport, not the marker.\n- In reduced mode:\n  - no marker layout/press spatial travel;\n  - no GSAP scale/rotation stamp;\n  - marker text and alternative list remain immediately readable;\n  - route is immediately complete;\n  - fog Lottie remains paused/hidden as current policy intends.\n- Current marker buttons have no component click handler or selected-marker state. Phase 2 should preserve native focus/activation only unless the product owner explicitly brings future marker-inspection behavior into scope.\n\n### Exact focused test additions\n\nBest dedicated location: new `src/components/player/workspace/VoyageChart.test.tsx`.\n\n1. `separates Motion marker layout from the GSAP visual`\n   - outer button owns Motion/layout;\n   - inner child alone owns `map-marker-new` transform/opacity;\n   - outer has no GSAP target;\n   - inner and outer are different nodes.\n\n2. `uses explicit location identity across insertion and update`\n   - rerender one → two locations;\n   - existing keyed marker retains DOM identity and focus;\n   - only supplied event key receives `map-marker-new`;\n   - changing array order does not change target;\n   - coordinate-less locations do not break target selection.\n\n3. `preserves marker focus and activation`\n   - role/name remain correct;\n   - keyboard focus works;\n   - click/Enter reaches the native button;\n   - decorative visual is `aria-hidden`;\n   - visible text alternative remains.\n\n4. `selects only the event route`\n   - multiple routes render;\n   - stable route keys exist;\n   - only explicit route key exposes `route-path`;\n   - target count is exactly one.\n\n5. `renders a readable nonspatial reduced state`\n   - no Motion press/layout travel;\n   - no hidden semantic content;\n   - location and route lists remain readable;\n   - fog animation does not become the only state signal.\n\nAdd to `PlayerExperience.test.tsx`:\n\n6. Start on Chart, emit `MAP_LOCATION_REVEALED` and `MAP_ROUTE_REVEALED`; assert the director receives the temporary progression host root, exact event/action ID, and never the permanent chart host.\n\n7. Assert cleanup/unmount restores focus and removes the event host without disturbing the chart host.\n\nShared-core integration tests:\n\n8. Mandatory two-host fixture:\n   - Host A `map-reveal`/`route-path`\n   - Host B `route-draw`/`route-path`\n   - only selected host mutates;\n   - exact target counts;\n   - metrics and cleanup are instance-local;\n   - no claim leak.\n\n9. A multi-day log therefore emits one required `log-entry-new` per day, violating exact cardinality and the Phase 2 rule against DOM-order identity.\n\n3. While a log event is active, `PlayerProgressionProp` mounts another `log-entry-new`, `PlayerExperience.tsx:1113-1124`. On the Log view, the broad host sees the overlay plus permanent-row targets.\n\n4. The authoritative new row does not exist until `refreshSnapshot()` after scene completion, `PlayerExperience.tsx:477-481`. The current production scene animates the temporary overlay, not the just-created Ship’s Log row. Existing permanent rows can accidentally satisfy or duplicate the contract.\n\n5. The temporary overlay has a second property collision: `.player-event-prop` supplies CSS `transform: translate(-50%, -50%)`, `player.css:2417-2425`, while GSAP targets that same outer node with `y`/transform.\n\n6. `data-gsap-owned` is only legacy metadata here. Motion ownership is not structurally declared on the row, so the existing ownership registry cannot reject the collision.\n\n**Required ownership structure**\n\nUse the frozen shared Phase 2 APIs, but preserve this DOM boundary:\n\n```text\nMotion motion.li row\n  stable semantic log content\n  decorative GSAP fresh-ink child: log-entry-new\n  decorative GSAP symbol child: log-symbol-new\n```\n\nConcrete rules:\n\n- `motion.li` owns `layout`/row transform and must have no scene part or GSAP ownership marker.\n- Move `log-entry-new` to a separate decorative inner child.\n- Keep `log-symbol-new` on its own child.\n- Bind the “new” target from an explicit progress-event ID/target handle, never `index`.\n- `PublicLogEntry.key` is the progress-event ID (`eventToLogEntry`, `ships-log.ts:160-179`), so it can match `ClientProgressEvent.id`.\n- Place stable text and the route-back button outside the GSAP opacity/transform child.\n- Split the event overlay similarly: CSS-positioned outer `.player-event-prop`; GSAP-owned inner ink visual.\n- Put the global event overlay and section enhancement in distinct hosts/instances. The global scene must resolve only its overlay host; the Log host may expose an optional section enhancement after authoritative state commits.\n- Do not make the authoritative row the required initial target until refresh/render ordering is changed; it is unavailable at initial playback.\n\n**Semantics, focus, and reduced motion**\n\n- Preserve the semantic `<ol>/<li>`, heading association, `<time>`, and route-back `<button>`.\n- Decorative ink and symbol children must be `aria-hidden`, pointer-inert, and contain no duplicated accessible text or IDs.\n- Insertion must not steal focus. Filtering must retain focus on the `<select>` and keep route buttons reachable.\n- Stable semantic content must remain readable while decorative GSAP children animate or fail.\n- Reduced mode must leave the row immediately readable and remove spatial travel; decoration may settle nearly instantly.\n- Wrapper CSS must preserve the existing two-column grid and mobile rule at `player.css:2039-2075,2740-2746`.\n- Final-state cleanup may revert the temporary decorative overlay only after authoritative content is committed; it must not hide or snap back the semantic row.\n\n**Exact focused test additions**\n\nPrefer a dedicated `src/components/player/workspace/ShipsLog.test.tsx`:\n\n1. Assert every `motion.li` structurally owns Motion layout and has no GSAP scene part/transform/opacity claim.\n3. Change filters; assert result stability, select focus retention, correct route buttons, and no fallback to “last row.”\n5. This is the exact shared-shell collision Phase 2 must remove. |\n| Artifact duplicate selectors | `artifact-reveal` exists in `ArtifactInspection.tsx:72`, `TreasureAltar.tsx:93`, and the temporary event prop in `PlayerExperience.tsx:1071`. It is therefore no longer literally undeclared in Phase 1’s contract, but remains semantically ambiguous: optional `0..2` does not identify header versus navigation, and the builder still raw-queries both. |\n| Ownership enforcement gap | The components use `data-gsap-owned`, but `ownership.ts:91-118,178-183` only recognizes `data-animation-owner`. Motion components also do not acquire/release registry claims. Current component annotations therefore do not enforce these ownership boundaries. |\n| Section-navigation focus | `PlayerExperience.tsx:913-916` uses document-wide `document.querySelector(\"[data-section-heading]\")`, so two Player hosts could focus the wrong section after companion navigation. |\n| Existing tests | `workspace-interactions.test.tsx:18-30` checks desktop/mobile activation; `:62-84` checks initial artifact focus, Escape, and focus return after unmount. It does not test Tab wrapping, early focus restoration on rerender, ownership rejection, host isolation, explicit dim targets, cleanup, or lifecycle cycles. `PlayerExperience.test.tsx:116-141` mocks all three relevant components, so it supplies no integrated coverage. |\n| Responsive state | `player.css:2279-2384` owns the dialog layout; `:2777-2789` changes it to a stacked sheet on narrow screens. It is still `96vw`/`92svh`, not the fully edge-to-edge narrow-screen sheet described in `docs/responsive-behavior.md:11`. |\n\n### Recommended Artifact structure\n\n```text\nMotion dialog-presence wrapper\n  inspection SceneHost / unique instance\n    Motion shared-layout artifact shell\n      stable artifact image/object\n      GSAP engraving child, decorative and aria-hidden\n      GSAP detail-light child, decorative and aria-hidden\n    semantic story card, immediately readable and never a GSAP target\n```\n\nConcrete migration rules:\n\n- Remove `artifact-reveal` and all GSAP ownership from the Motion shared shell.\n- Claim Motion ownership structurally for the shell’s `layout`/`transform`; diagnostic attributes alone are insufficient.\n- Move `artifact-engraving` to a dedicated inner SVG/group or decorative child. Give GSAP only `clip-path` or stroke properties.\n- Give the existing pointer/detail light its own explicit target if retained.\n- Keep the meaningful story card outside GSAP clipping and readable before animation completes.\n- Use a dialog-local SceneHost/instance so two open inspection fixtures with identical part names remain isolated.\n- Do not use GSAP FLIP on either Motion `layoutId` node.\n- Coordinate the matching source-shell boundary with `TreasureAltar.tsx`; it must not remain a generic artifact-award destination.\n- If `artifact-inspection` is promoted to production, invoke its inner-detail sequence only after focus enters the dialog. Otherwise retain it truthfully as an architecture-ready future contract; do not imply the engraving runs in production.\n\n### Recommended Companion structure\n\n```text\nCSS journal-readiness wrapper\n  Motion-owned header layout\n    semantic controls\n    pointer-inert, aria-hidden GSAP header-dim overlay\n\n  Motion-owned desktop navigation layout\n    semantic buttons\n    pointer-inert, aria-hidden GSAP desktop-navigation-dim overlay\n\nMobile companion wrapper\n  Motion-owned mobile navigation layout\n    semantic buttons\n    pointer-inert, aria-hidden GSAP mobile-navigation-dim overlay\n```\n\nReplace `peripheral` with explicit external handles/targets such as:\n\n- `companion-header-dim`\n- `companion-desktop-navigation-dim`\n- `companion-mobile-navigation-dim`\n\nEach should have deliberate `0..1` cardinality, GSAP opacity ownership, and no interactive descendants. Header/nav transforms, layout, focus, and pointer behavior remain Motion/React-owned. Chapter release should animate overlay opacity back to its transparent baseline, not scale or fade the semantic roots.\n\nBecause these surfaces will be outside a local chapter SceneHost, the chapter scene should receive registered external target handles from the frozen Phase 2 API. It must not rediscover them through the whole Player root or DOM order. Remove generic `peripheral` from both `chapter-release` and the inherited future chapter-subscene contracts.\n\n### Accessibility and interaction constraints\n\n- Initial focus enters the close button; Tab and Shift+Tab wrap even if focus was displaced outside the dialog.\n- Escape closes once and prevents unintended propagation.\n- Focus returns after Motion exit to the exact connected, enabled artifact trigger.\n- The current inline `close={() => ...}` changes identity on parent rerenders; because the focus effect depends on `close`, cleanup can restore focus early. Use a stable callback or stable event ref.\n- Use an instance-unique dialog label ID rather than fixed `artifact-name`.\n- Background content must not re-enter the focus order while the modal is active.\n- Decorative GSAP engraving/light/overlay children must be `aria-hidden` and noninteractive.\n- Companion buttons remain enabled, tabbable, clickable, and visually readable during dimming and cleanup.\n- Mobile navigation should receive the same `aria-current=\"page\"` semantics as desktop navigation.\n- Reduced mode must expose the same readable dialog, connection text, active section, and unseen state without spatial travel.\n- Resolve post-navigation heading focus inside the active Player host, not with `document.querySelector`.\n\n### Focused tests required\n\n- Artifact DOM ownership: Motion shell and GSAP engraving are distinct nodes; GSAP shell transform claims are rejected.\n- Artifact two-host isolation: identical engraving part names, only the requested host changes.\n- No inspection-rendered `artifact-reveal` target and no whole-object GSAP FLIP.\n- Initial focus, full Tab/Shift+Tab trap, Escape, backdrop/close button, stable rerender, and exact trigger return after exit.\n- Decorative children excluded from the accessibility tree; semantic card immediately readable.\n- Twenty inspection open/close cycles return host, claim, listener, timer, and detached-node counts to baseline.\n- Companion target inventory contains no generic `peripheral`; every dim surface is explicit and unique.\n- During chapter release, only overlay nodes receive GSAP writes; every navigation control remains interactive and focused state is preserved.\n- Success, skip, interruption, unmount, and reduced fallback restore overlay baseline and release claims.\n- Desktop, phone portrait, and short-landscape fixtures assert the intended visible overlay set and no duplicate cardinality.\n- Viewport checks at all mandated sizes cover dialog reachability, no horizontal overflow, unclipped close control, stable focus order, and mobile navigation.\n- Registry tests must preserve truthful reachability totals if `artifact-inspection` remains future-contract, or update caller evidence and totals if deliberately promoted.\n\n### Dependencies and uncertainty\n\n- Blocked on the coordinator-owned SceneHost/instance resolver, qualified-target delivery, external-handle API, and structural Motion ownership hook.\n- `scene-utils.ts` currently re-queries raw DOM targets; until the core lane supplies only preflight-qualified targets, rejected/hidden optional targets can still receive writes.\n- `PlayerExperience.tsx`, `scene-registry.ts`, `story.scene.ts`, `TreasureAltar.tsx`, and global player styles require single-owner coordination.\n- Product decision still needed: connect `artifact-inspection` as an inner-detail production scene after focus, or keep it as a truthful future contract. The specification permits retaining the inner sequence “if useful” but forbids whole-object GSAP either way.\n\nConfidence: high for static ownership, selector, focus, and test-gap findings; medium for the final `artifact-inspection` reachability choice because the governing text intentionally leaves that disposition conditional."}},{"agent_name":"/root/r6_access_final_state","agent_status":"running"},{"agent_name":"/root/r7_pageflip_boundary","agent_status":"running"},{"agent_name":"/root/r8_scene_inventory","agent_status":"running"},{"agent_name":"/root/r9_requirements_reconcile","agent_status":"running"},{"agent_name":"/root/w1_phase1_bounded_paint","agent_status":{"completed":"W1 is stopped and read-only.\n\n- Initial hashes matched, so there was no ownership conflict.\n- Added a 100 ms watchdog to `nextPaint`, with timeout cleanup on paint or abort.\n- Added two regressions for suspended rAF progression and abort cleanup.\n- Focused Vitest run passed: 1 file, 14/14 tests, exit 0.\n- The test run preceded only the final behavior-neutral extraction of `100` into `paintAcquisitionTimeoutMs`; that last refactor was not rerun.\n- Read-only inspection confirms the patch is syntactically complete.\n- No files outside the two assigned paths were edited; no Git or runtime state was changed."}}]}

R10 is complete and reviewed. It confirms the final mutation/browser gate must be the repository’s nonce-backed `npm run validate` wrapper; raw `npm run test:e2e` is not accepted for mutating evidence. It also mapped every Phase 2 test requirement and confirmed the current gaps: no host tests, no property-group/write-permit proof, incomplete PageFlip identity/accessibility, no mandatory two-host test, only three Journal lifecycle cycles instead of five surfaces × twenty, and screenshots without the required viewport assertions.

R10’s slot is released. Only the read-only design-record draft remains active; all runtime, database, ports, build, browser, Git, and synchronization resources stay untouched by Phase 2 while the monitored Phase 1 task repairs its three browser failures.

**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T21:38:39.238Z

R11 is complete. The design-record draft now covers all 20 required frozen concepts and reconciles the finished SceneHost, ownership, component, PageFlip, final-state, validation, and 458-record traceability findings. No Phase 2 files have been written yet because the shared checkout is still owned by Phase 1.

All Phase 2 discovery lanes are now complete and their slots released. I’ve reviewed every result; the ready critical path after handoff is:

1. re-read the finalized Phase 1 diff/reports and fast-forward the preserved Phase 2 branch;
2. write/freeze the design record;
3. implement the shared registries/resolver/ownership/Director contracts under one core writer;
4. fan out verified non-overlapping component/PageFlip/documentation writers;
5. integrate and run the single isolated validation gate.

The monitored Phase 1 task is still active. Its focused timing test is now inside a corrected 4.0–4.5s synthetic envelope while the real browser acceptance retains the 5–10s end-to-end requirement; it also hit generated `.next` type artifacts from an earlier interrupted dev process and is resolving only that generated state.

## Assistant
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T21:47:07.390Z

The latest Phase 1 gate proved the timing repair and now passes **46 files / 300 tests**. Three later browser assertions remain: legacy Journal opening did not reach the chapter heading, one animation listener remained after fresh-document unmount, and one fallback assertion read a deliberately absent event-payload field instead of the rendered persisted objective. Its three diagnosis lanes are active; four downstream cases remain gated.

While that finishes, the Phase 2 zero-loss mapping is independent and read-only, so I’m using the idle capacity now.

### Subagent wave 2 started — requirement mapping

All eight lanes are read-only. They may inspect only the OA source, matrix, audit, roadmap, test plan, Phase 2 brief, and relevant source evidence; no files, Git, server, browser, database, tests, dependencies, or synchronization may change.

| Lane | Role / status | Owned requirement range | End goal / deliverable | Non-scope | Dependencies / integration | Expected benefit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | Gateway/access mapper — Active | OA-001–OA-047 | CSV-ready exact/combined/partial/new mapping, phase, status, host/ownership/target dependencies | No OA-048+, no edits | I reconcile with R9 schema and Phase 4 handoff | Parallelizes gateway/sign-in/invitation reconciliation |
| M2 | Library/journal-entry mapper — Active | OA-048–OA-090 | Same fields for Player Library, Waiting Room, Journal introduction | No other ranges | Feeds immutable 238-row ledger | Removes a large non-code documentation lane |
| M3 | Journal/chapter mapper — Active | OA-091–OA-121 | Same fields for Journal pages and Chapter release | No other ranges | Cross-checks current PageFlip/chapter contracts | Protects high-risk scene assignments |
| M4 | Chart/altar mapper — Active | OA-122–OA-150 | Same fields for Voyage Chart and Treasure Altar | No other ranges | Reuses reviewed R4/R5 findings | Converts broad rows into exact preserved mappings |
| M5 | Quest/log mapper — Active | OA-151–OA-174 | Same fields for Side Quest and Ship’s Log | No other ranges | Reuses R4/R7 collision evidence | Covers PageFlip and log identity requirements |
| M6 | Finale/Captain mapper — Active | OA-175–OA-206 | Same fields for Finale and Captain Library | No other ranges | Distinguishes Phase 2 architecture from later visuals | Prevents false implementation claims |
| M7 | Quartermaster/Studio mapper — Active | OA-207–OA-238 | Same fields for Quartermaster and Studio | No other ranges | Reuses R6 ownership and Phase 4 dnd-kit handoff | Covers command and drag ownership boundaries |
| M8 | Codex/schema verifier — Active | All 220 Codex IDs; schema only for OA | Normalize phase/status/dependencies and verify merged totals without changing source text | No OA per-row mapping, no edits | Independently validates M1–M7 output against R9’s 458 denominator | Adds a cross-check before the ledger writer starts |

Eight workers are active; five slots remain open. The coordinator retains the complete request, mapping merge, contradictions, exact totals, all writers, shared resources, validation, Git, documentation integration, and final synchronization.

## Assistant
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T21:51:43.957Z

### M8 child lanes started

M8 split its independent normalization and schema work to shorten the zero-loss critical path.

| Lane | Role | Status | Mode / ownership | End goal | Non-scope | Integration | Expected benefit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| M8A | Codex matrix normalizer | Active | Read-only; exactly 220 Codex CSV rows | Per-row normalized current status/phase/dependencies without altering historical fields | No OA mapping, edits, tests, Git, runtime, or docs writes | M8 reviews and merges it with the validator result | Parallelizes the largest homogeneous row set |
| M8B | Traceability schema validator | Active | Read-only; brief/audit/roadmap/test-plan/reconciliation/Phase 1 docs | Validator rules for prefixes, denominators, mappings, normalized states, and zero-unmapped proof | No per-row normalization, edits, tests, Git, runtime, or sync | M8 reconciles it against M8A and M1–M7 | Independent loss/consistency check |

There are now **10 active mapping workers plus the coordinator**, with **3 slots available**. M8 retains cross-check and integration; no ownership conflict exists because all work is read-only.

Early mapping truth is already sharper: OA-048–OA-090 contains 43 rows, including six likely intentional new matrix rows; gateway/access/invitation requirements also contain many specific microstates hidden by broad rows. These are being preserved as partial/new mappings, not falsely labeled implemented.

no continuous ornamental motion\",\"Each file reports truthful independent progress and failure; retry/removal preserves other rows and focus\",\"MX-116; TaleEditor only sets generic Uploading N files and final state\",\"No per-file progress source, rows or animation tests\",\"Phase 2 enables safe layout wrappers; no replay\",\"Immediate restored value and control availability\",\"Undo/redo identifies the affected edit, updates disabled states immediately and never implies a server-persisted result before autosave succeeds\",\"TaleEditor has past/future stacks and functional undo/redo buttons but no visual transition\",\"No affected-object mapping, animation or dedicated matrix row\",\"Functional history is not animation implementation; Motion may own separate post-drop settle child\",\"Stable chapter ID and destination index\",\"dnd-kit + Motion\",\"Chapter drag/keyboard reorder completes and persists\",\"Every successful reorder\",\"Immediate final order and accessible position announcement\",\"Chapter order persists, keyboard and pointer paths agree, and no Motion/GSAP writer touches the dnd transform\",\"Current TaleEditor uses dnd-kit for blocks; MX-111 explicitly covers block reorder settle\",\"No chapter sortable implementation or matrix row\",\"A new row is mandatory so block reorder cannot falsely satisfy chapter reorder\",\"High\"\n\"OA-235\",\"Block insertion\",\"MX-109\",\"Exact conceptual row\",\"architecture_blocked\",\"architecture_ready\",\"Roadmap Phase 4\",\"Phase 4 Bring the Harbor Alive\",\"Motion presence wrapper;
