ceremony handoff; Phase 5 asset fallback if used\",\"platform-ceremony for bounded lock animation\",\"Motion owns edition/result shell; GSAP owns bounded lock child; future Rive owns internal object\",\"Exact immutable version ID/label and lock target; editable draft excluded\",\"Motion + bounded GSAP; optional future Rive internal\",\"Published immutable edition becomes current after authoritative response\",\"Once per edition creation; optional presentation-only replay\",\"Immediate immutable badge and disabled edit semantics\",\"Lock appears only for an authoritative immutable edition, never disables the editable draft incorrectly, and leaves the version label readable\",\"MX-115 broadly mentions immutable badge; current version list uses static current state\",\"Broad row can hide the specific lock animation; no lock target, host or tests\",\"A distinct row prevents publication ceremony from falsely satisfying immutable-edition locking\",\"High\"\n```\n\nReview notes:\n\n- Quartermaster OA-207–OA-220 needs one architecture owner for the dedicated `quartermaster-command` host, receipt/final-state handoff and claim lifetime. Ordinary controls, recent rows and command cards must remain outside GSAP selection.\n- OA-216–OA-219 belong to Phase 3 for their Player-facing effects, even though the Quartermaster command is the authoritative initiating action.\n- Studio OA-221–OA-238 stays Phase 4. Phase 2 should establish only the shared ownership API and dnd-kit boundaries.\n- dnd-kit must exclusively own drag transforms. Motion may animate surrounding presence/layout or a separate post-drop child; GSAP must not touch draggable transforms.\n- Phase 2 exit statuses are recommendations contingent on successful implementation and validation. They are not current-state claims.\n\nUncertainty: current mapping and implementation evidence are high confidence. Quartermaster visual placement in Phase 4 is medium confidence because the roadmap needs an explicit Quartermaster future-visual assignment rather than relying on inference.\n\n"}},{"agent_name":"/root/m8_codex_schema_verify","agent_status":"running"},{"agent_name":"/root/m8_codex_schema_verify/matrix_normalizer","agent_status":"running"},{"agent_name":"/root/m8_codex_schema_verify/schema_validator","agent_status":"running"},{"agent_name":"/root/r11_design_record_draft","agent_status":{"completed":"# Project Lanternwake Phase 2 Design Record\n\nStatus: Draft for coordinator review; shared architecture is implementation-ready, but the record must not be marked approved until the Phase 1 handoff and the unresolved gates in Section 16 are reconciled.  \nPhase: Phase 2 — Claim the Deck  \nFormal scope: Runtime Ownership, Scene Scoping, and Animation Boundary Enforcement  \nRepository: `Kgray44/treasurehuntSoT`  \nObserved design snapshot: `main` at `19b87c3c187c1d1c2feb8a7e51bea02b66858003`, with concurrent uncommitted Phase 1 and unrelated work preserved  \nPhase 1 status: Interfaces and focused tests exist; Phase 1 is not yet closed because browser/full validation, reports, synchronization, and the final handoff remain pending.\n\n## 1. Decision summary\n\nPhase 2 establishes two invariants:\n\n1. A production scene can mutate only targets resolved inside its registered `SceneHost`, except for registry-minted external handles named by its contract.\n2. A runtime can mutate a DOM property only while holding a current write permit for that element’s normalized property group.\n\nApplication-root querying is unsafe because the Player and Quartermaster roots currently contain permanent section content, temporary event overlays, dialogs, navigation, controls, and repeated part names. Broad queries can therefore select another scene’s object, both the permanent and temporary version of an object, a hidden PageFlip source, or a stale clone. DOM order cannot repair that ambiguity.\n\nPhase 2 uses provider-scoped host and ownership registries, immutable scene-instance identities, one canonical resolved target set, property-group claims, permit-gated writers, explicit final-state handoffs, and lifecycle-safe cleanup. Diagnostic attributes remain useful evidence but never grant ownership or target eligibility.\n\nThe architecture supports many registered and nested hosts concurrently. Phase 2 does not promise multiple simultaneous active GSAP timelines inside the existing single Director; it promises that registered hosts remain isolated and that playing one scene cannot affect another host. True concurrent playback may be added later without changing host, target, or ownership identity.\n\n## 2. Verified starting state and Phase 1 compatibility boundary\n\nPhase 1 currently supplies stable equivalents of:\n\n- `SceneTargetContract`\n- `SceneTargetRequirement`\n- `SceneTargetObservation`\n- `ScenePreflightReport`\n- `PresentationOutcome`\n- `PresentationReceipt`\n- `ScenePlaybackPolicy`\n- `SceneAcknowledgmentPolicy`\n- `SceneFinalStatePolicy`\n- `ResolvedMotionPolicy`\n- `JournalPhaseOutcome`\n- `ReplayablePresentation`\n- `SceneReachability`\n\nThe current implementation also has explicit contracts for 28 scenes and documented focused evidence of 297 passing unit/component tests. This does not close the Phase 1 dependency gate.\n\nConfirmed Phase 2 gaps are:\n\n- no `SceneHost` registry or component exists;\n- `AnimationDirector.play()` accepts a raw root plus string host metadata;\n- host IDs are not registered or uniqueness-enforced;\n- builders re-query the DOM after preflight, so the inspected, claimed, and animated sets can differ;\n- a parent query can descend into nested hosts;\n- optional ownership rejection does not prevent a raw builder query from animating the rejected node;\n- the ownership registry is module-global, exact-property-only, non-enumerable, and not scope-aware;\n- Motion, CSS, PageFlip, Rive, Lottie, and dnd-kit do not participate through enforceable leases;\n- hidden PageFlip sources remain part of raw cardinality and raw builder selection;\n- held GSAP contexts and ownership claims are not one lifecycle contract;\n- broad Player and Quartermaster roots remain compatibility boundaries rather than dedicated scene hosts.\n\nPhase 2 extends these Phase 1 contracts. It does not introduce a competing receipt, motion-policy, acknowledgment, or fallback system.\n\n## 3. Frozen architecture definitions\n\n### 3.1 Shared identity types\n\n```ts\ntype Brand<Value, Name extends string> = Value & {\n  readonly __brand: Name;\n};\n\nexport type SceneHostId = Brand<string, \"SceneHostId\">;\nexport type SceneHostRegistrationId = Brand<string, \"SceneHostRegistrationId\">;\nexport type SceneInstanceId = Brand<string, \"SceneInstanceId\">;\nexport type SceneTargetId = Brand<string, \"SceneTargetId\">;\nexport type ExternalSceneTargetHandleId = Brand<string, \"ExternalSceneTargetHandleId\">;\nexport type OwnershipClaimId = Brand<string, \"OwnershipClaimId\">;\n\nexport type ScenePlaybackIdentity = Readonly<{\n  instanceId: SceneInstanceId;\n  hostId: SceneHostId;\n  hostRegistrationId: SceneHostRegistrationId;\n  sceneName: AnimationSceneName;\n  eventOrActionId?: string;\n  playback: \"live\" | \"replay\";\n  invocationSequence: number;\n}>;\n```\n\nA `SceneHostId` identifies a semantic mounted boundary. A registration ID identifies one exact mount generation. A `SceneInstanceId` identifies one invocation inside that generation.\n\nLive playback and replay preserve the same authoritative event or action ID but always receive different instance IDs and invocation sequences. Scene name, timestamp, host name, current section, and React array indexes are insufficient identities.\n\nIDs are opaque. Story text, invitation codes, credentials, payload content, and user-entered values must never be encoded in them.\n\n### 3.2 Frozen item 1 — `SceneHost`\n\n`SceneHost` is the React boundary that registers one DOM root with the provider-scoped registry and exposes a controller to its descendants and authorized callers.\n\n```ts\nexport type SceneHostController = Readonly<{\n  registration: SceneHostRegistration;\n  beginScene(input: SceneHostActivation): SceneHostHandle;\n  createExternalTarget(input: ExternalTargetRegistration): ExternalSceneTargetHandle;\n  release(reason: SceneHostReleaseReason): SceneHostCleanupReceipt;\n}>;\n```\n\nA host registration may survive many sequential live and replay invocations. It owns at most one current active or held instance unless a later explicit concurrency contract is introduced.\n\n### 3.3 Frozen item 2 — `SceneHostKind`\n\n```ts\nexport type SceneHostKind =\n  | \"gateway\"\n  | \"access\"\n  | \"player-progression\"\n  | \"player-section-enhancement\"\n  | \"journal-opening\"\n  | \"quartermaster-command\"\n  | \"platform-ceremony\"\n  | \"development-showcase\";\n```\n\n`first-arrival` and `session-reentry` may declare both `gateway` and `journal-opening`. Access/login playback uses `access`; signed-in Quartermaster command playback uses `quartermaster-command`. Temporary Player event overlays use `player-progression`; permanent chart, log, artifact, and similar section-local surfaces use `player-section-enhancement`.\n\n### 3.4 Frozen item 3 — `SceneHostRegistration`\n\n```ts\nexport type SceneHostRegistration = Readonly<{\n  registrationId: SceneHostRegistrationId;\n  hostId: SceneHostId;\n  kind: SceneHostKind;\n  root: HTMLElement;\n  providerId: string;\n  generation: number;\n  registeredAt: number;\n}>;\n```\n\nRegistration deliberately does not contain a mutable scene instance. Combining mounted-host identity and invocation identity would let a stale handle silently refer to a later invocation.\n\nHost IDs must be unique inside one provider registry. React Strict Mode register/unregister cycles use exact registration tokens and idempotent cleanup. Reusing a semantic host ID after unmount produces a new registration generation.\n\n### 3.5 Frozen item 4 — `SceneHostHandle`\n\n```ts\nexport type SceneHostHandle = Readonly<{\n  identity: ScenePlaybackIdentity;\n  registration: SceneHostRegistration;\n  contract: SceneTargetContractV2;\n  ownershipScope: OwnershipScope;\n  motionPolicy: ResolvedMotionPolicy;\n  presentationState: ScenePresentationState;\n  resolveTargets(): SceneTargetResolution;\n  resolvePart(part: ScenePartName): readonly SceneTargetHandle[];\n  resolveExternalTarget(handle: ExternalSceneTargetHandle): SceneTargetResolution;\n  addCleanup(cleanup: () => void): void;\n  handoff(request: SceneFinalStateHandoff): Promise<SceneHandoffReceipt>;\n  release(reason: SceneReleaseReason): SceneHostCleanupReceipt;\n}>;\n```\n\nA handle is immutable and invocation-specific. A replay or later live play cannot mutate it into the new instance.\n\n### 3.6 Frozen items 5 and 6 — `SceneInstanceId` and `SceneHostId`\n\n`SceneInstanceId` is registry-minted from a provider nonce plus a monotonic invocation sequence. The scene, host, event/action, live/replay status, and sequence remain separate typed fields rather than being parsed from the ID.\n\n`SceneHostId` is supplied or generated for the semantic boundary and is validated against the registered root. Neither identifier is trusted merely because a matching `data-*` attribute exists.\n\n### 3.7 Frozen item 7 — `SceneTargetHandle`\n\n```ts\nexport type SceneTargetHandle = Readonly<{\n  targetId: SceneTargetId;\n  part: ScenePartName;\n  hostId: SceneHostId;\n  hostRegistrationId: SceneHostRegistrationId;\n  sceneInstanceId: SceneInstanceId;\n  runtime: AnimationRuntimeOwner;\n  allowedProperties: readonly AnimatedProperty[];\n  permit: AnimationWritePermit;\n}>;\n```\n\nThe registry retains the internal weak element reference. General scene code receives handles, not arbitrary elements. Approved runtime adapters may unwrap a current handle after validating its permit.\n\nA target handle becomes stale when its element disconnects, its host generation changes, its scene instance closes, its external lifetime expires, its permit is released, or its abort signal settles.\n\n### 3.8 Frozen item 8 — `ExternalSceneTargetHandle`\n\n```ts\nexport type ExternalSceneTargetHandle = Readonly<{\n  handleId: ExternalSceneTargetHandleId;\n  targetId: SceneTargetId;\n  owningHostId: SceneHostId;\n  owningHostRegistrationId: SceneHostRegistrationId;\n  allowedConsumerHostId: SceneHostId;\n  owningRuntime: AnimationRuntimeOwner;\n  allowedProperties: readonly AnimatedProperty[];\n  lifetime: \"scene\" | \"handoff\";\n  sourceInstanceId?: SceneInstanceId;\n}>;\n```\n\nThe element and unforgeable token remain private to the registry.\n\nExternal handles replace broad cross-tree selectors for artifact source/destination, chart markers and routes, log insertion, shared-layout continuity, and later progression handoffs. Resolution revalidates token, exact element identity, owner host generation, consumer host, property subset, connection, visibility, PageFlip status, lifetime, and source instance.\n\nRaw `HTMLElement` external options are invalid. A local element cannot be routed through an external handle to bypass its host contract. DOM order never selects an artifact, marker, route, quest, or log entry.\n\n### 3.9 Frozen item 9 — `OwnershipScope`\n\n```ts\nexport type OwnershipScope =\n  | Readonly<{\n      kind: \"scene\";\n      scopeId: string;\n      hostId: SceneHostId;\n      sceneInstanceId: SceneInstanceId;\n    }>\n  | Readonly<{\n      kind: \"component\";\n      scopeId: string;\n      hostId: SceneHostId;\n      componentInstanceId: string;\n    }>\n  | Readonly<{\n      kind: \"drag\";\n      scopeId: string;\n      hostId: SceneHostId;\n      dragInstanceId: string;\n    }>;\n```\n\nScene scopes own cinematic work. Component scopes own mounted Motion, PageFlip, Rive, Lottie, or active CSS runtime surfaces. Drag scopes own dnd-kit transforms for one pointer or keyboard drag.\n\n### 3.10 Frozen item 10 — `AnimationOwnershipClaim`\n\n```ts\nexport type AnimationOwnershipClaim = Readonly<{\n  target: SceneTargetId | Element;\n  runtime: AnimationRuntimeOwner;\n  properties: readonly AnimatedProperty[];\n  scope: OwnershipScope;\n  part?: ScenePartName;\n  signal?: AbortSignal;\n}>;\n```\n\nThe provider-scoped ownership registry is keyed by element identity and normalized property group. Required claims are acquired atomically: either every required group is granted or none is committed.\n\n### 3.11 Frozen item 11 — `AnimationOwnershipClaimResult`\n\n```ts\nexport type AnimationOwnershipClaimResult =\n  | Readonly<{\n      status: \"granted\";\n      claimId: OwnershipClaimId;\n      permit: AnimationWritePermit;\n      release(reason: OwnershipReleaseReason): AnimationOwnershipReleaseResult;\n    }>\n  | Readonly<{\n      status: \"rejected\";\n      property: AnimatedProperty;\n      propertyGroup: PropertyOwnershipGroup;\n      requestedOwner: AnimationRuntimeOwner;\n      existingOwner?: AnimationRuntimeOwner;\n      existingSceneInstanceId?: SceneInstanceId;\n      reason: AnimationOwnershipConflictReason;\n    }>;\n```\n\nA rejected result has no permit. Runtime adapters cannot write without a current permit. A warning or diagnostic attribute cannot override rejection.\n\nSame runtime, same scope, same group is a compatible reentrant claim with an independent claim ID/refcount. Same runtime in a different scope conflicts, because two GSAP scenes can fight just as two different runtimes can. Non-overlapping groups and different elements remain compatible.\n\n### 3.12 Frozen item 12 — `AnimatedProperty`\n\n```ts\nexport type AnimatedProperty =\n  | \"transform\"\n  | \"translate\"\n  | \"scale\"\n  | \"rotate\"\n  | \"opacity\"\n  | \"layout\"\n  | \"clip-path\"\n  | \"filter\"\n  | \"width\"\n  | \"height\"\n  | \"path-drawing\"\n  | \"stroke-dasharray\"\n  | \"stroke-dashoffset\"\n  | \"scroll-position\"\n  | \"visibility\"\n  | `custom-property:--${string}`;\n```\n\nUnqualified `custom` is retired because it cannot distinguish unrelated CSS variables.\n\nDOM-owning runtimes are:\n\n```ts\ntype AnimationRuntimeOwner =\n  | \"gsap\"\n  | \"motion\"\n  | \"css\"\n  | \"page-flip\"\n  | \"rive\"\n  | \"lottie\"\n  | \"web-animations\"\n  | \"react\"\n  | \"dnd-kit\";\n```\n\nWeb Audio may remain in scene metadata but is not a DOM property owner.\n\n### 3.13 Frozen item 13 — `PropertyOwnershipGroup`\n\n| Animated properties | Normalized group |\n|---|---|\n| `transform`, `translate`, `scale`, `rotate`, `layout` | `transform` |\n| `opacity` | `opacity` |\n| `clip-path` | `clip-path` |\n| `filter` | `filter` |\n| `width`, `height` | `size` |\n| `path-drawing`, `stroke-dasharray`, `stroke-dashoffset` | `path-drawing` |\n| `scroll-position` | `scroll-position` |\n| `visibility` | `visibility` |\n| `custom-property:--name` | the exact custom-property key |\n\nThe same-element pair rules are:\n\n- GSAP/Motion, Motion/CSS, GSAP/CSS: reject overlapping active groups.\n- Motion `layoutId`/GSAP transform: always reject on the same node.\n- Motion/PageFlip or GSAP/PageFlip: reject transform/layout/size on PageFlip’s physical surface.\n- GSAP/Rive and GSAP/Lottie: reject reserved groups on the runtime’s inner surface; animate a neutral outer wrapper instead.\n- dnd-kit/Motion and dnd-kit/GSAP: reject transform overlap during drag.\n- Separate wrappers or non-overlapping groups are the compatible form.\n\nStatic CSS values do not claim a group. An active keyframe or transition does, and its class must not activate after rejection.\n\n### 3.14 Frozen item 14 — `SceneFinalStateHandoff`\n\n```ts\nexport type SceneFinalStateHandoff =\n  | { kind: \"revert-immediately\" }\n  | { kind: \"hold-final-until-unmount\"; semanticState: string }\n  | {\n      kind: \"commit-final-state\";\n      semanticState: string;\n      verifyCommitted: () => boolean | Promise<boolean>;\n    }\n  | {\n      kind: \"reconcile-then-revert\";\n      semanticState: string;\n      handoffTargetId: SceneTargetId;\n      reconcile: () => boolean | Promise<boolean>;\n    }\n  | {\n      kind: \"fallback-to-static-state\";\n      semanticState: string;\n      fallback: string;\n    };\n```\n\nPhase 1 compatibility mapping is:\n\n| Phase 1 name | Phase 2 canonical name |\n|---|---|\n| `revert-immediately` | `revert-immediately` |\n| `hold-until-unmount` | `hold-final-until-unmount` |\n| `commit-semantic-pose` | `commit-final-state` |\n| `reconcile-then-revert` | `reconcile-then-revert` |\n| `readable-static-fallback` | `fallback-to-static-state` |\n\nA held final pose retains its GSAP context, scene instance, and ownership leases. It cannot keep the visual pose while releasing ownership. A commit or reconciliation verifies that semantic UI has assumed the final state before cinematic properties revert. Failure uses a typed handoff failure and an approved stable fallback; cleanup never silently exposes the old locked/closed pose.\n\nReceipts record:\n\n- final-state policy;\n- whether state committed;\n- handoff target ID;\n- handoff start/completion/failure;\n- cleanup start/completion/result.\n\n### 3.15 Frozen item 15 — `SceneCleanupPolicy`\n\nAll cleanup is idempotent, exact-scope, reverse-registration-order, and continue-on-error.\n\nTerminal cleanup order is:\n\n1. stop, kill, cancel, or destroy the active writer;\n2. run registered scene callbacks in reverse order;\n3. commit, reconcile, revert, or transfer the context to a held handoff;\n4. release exact ownership leases in reverse order;\n5. invalidate consumed external handles;\n6. release the scene instance;\n7. remove instance attributes only if the current registration token still matches;\n8. on host release, release active and held instances before unregistering the host.\n\nOn preflight or runtime failure, the failed scene releases its leases before invoking fallback. The fallback receives its own scope and claims.\n\nRelease is mandatory after success, failure, user or policy skip, abort, interruption, timeout, route change, builder/timeline failure, fallback, runtime failure, and unmount. `release()` can never clear a sibling scope’s claim.\n\n### 3.16 Frozen item 16 — `PageFlipSourceBoundary`\n\n```ts\nexport type PageFlipSourceBoundary = Readonly<{\n  pageFlipInstanceId: string;\n  generation: number;\n  role: \"hidden-source\";\n  inert: true;\n  ariaHidden: true;\n  targetEligible: false;\n}>;\n```\n\nThe hidden React source tree is never a cinematic target, never contributes to cardinality, never receives current instance identity, is inert and absent from the accessibility tree, and cannot retain duplicate IDs or leases.\n\n### 3.17 Frozen item 17 — `PageFlipCloneBoundary`\n\n```ts\nexport type PageFlipCloneBoundary = Readonly<{\n  pageFlipInstanceId: string;\n  pageId: string;\n  generation: number;\n  role: \"primary-visible\" | \"temporary-curl\";\n  lifecycle: \"current\" | \"off-page\" | \"turning\" | \"stale\";\n  targetEligible: boolean;\n}>;\n```\n\nOnly a current, primary, deliberate visible target may qualify. Off-page, temporary-curl, stale-generation, detached, hidden-source, and unregistered nodes remain diagnostic-only.\n\nPrimary pages carry stable `FlipBookPage.id`, instance, generation, and lifecycle identity. Supplied primary elements are tracked by object identity; StPageFlip-created soft-turn clones are identified as temporary, made inert/`aria-hidden`, and stripped of cinematic eligibility.\n\nIDs and IDREFs must be namespaced or rewritten on primary visible pages and removed from hidden and temporary copies while preserving accessibility relationships. Current logical page identity is preserved by page ID across content updates and orientation changes, not only by numeric index.\n\nStPageFlip owns physical surface transform/layout/size on its actual wrapper and page nodes. GSAP may animate explicit narrative children before a turn or after commit/settle, never the curl. Lifecycle labels are `turn-start`, `turn-commit`, and `turn-settle`.\n\n`manual-page-flip` and `programmatic-page-flip` remain deprecated traceability tombstones but lose executable fake-curl Director/showcase behavior. Manual, keyboard, pointer, and programmatic turns invoke the real PageFlip runtime.\n\n### 3.18 Frozen item 18 — scene-host diagnostics\n\nDevelopment/test diagnostics expose sanitized:\n\n- Project Lanternwake phase;\n- scene, instance, host, registration, and host kind;\n- opaque event/action correlation ID;\n- runtime and ownership scope;\n- target part and opaque target ID;\n- required/optional status;\n- visibility, source, stale, cardinality, and ownership result;\n- final-state policy and handoff result;\n- cleanup result;\n- active host, instance, handle, and claim counts.\n\nCanonical target failures are:\n\n```text\ntarget-not-found\ntarget-outside-host\ntarget-hidden\ntarget-zero-box\ntarget-duplicate\ntarget-stale-instance\ntarget-source-tree\ntarget-wrong-owner\ntarget-disconnected\ntarget-contract-mismatch\n```\n\nHandoff and cleanup additionally use `handoff-failed` and `cleanup-failed`.\n\nDiagnostics and serializable receipts never contain DOM references. The diagnostic claim index stores metadata plus `WeakRef<Element>` and prunes before reporting. Finalization may assist cleanup but is never correctness-critical.\n\nNever log invitation codes, PINs, passwords, tokens, secret story content, unpublished Creator content, private payloads, or user-entered credentials. Production users receive readable fallback behavior, not internal diagnostics.\n\n### 3.19 Frozen item 19 — existing-scene compatibility plan\n\nPhase 2 introduces `SceneTargetContractV2`:\n\n```ts\nexport type SceneTargetRequirementV2 =\n  | (SceneTargetRequirementBase & {\n      source: \"host\";\n      part: ScenePartName;\n    })\n  | (SceneTargetRequirementBase & {\n      source: \"external\";\n      part: ScenePartName;\n      externalKey: string;\n    });\n\nexport type SceneTargetContractV2 = Readonly<{\n  version: 2;\n  sceneName: AnimationSceneName;\n  reachability: SceneReachabilityV2;\n  expectedHostKinds: readonly SceneHostKind[];\n  requiredTargets: readonly SceneTargetRequirementV2[];\n  optionalTargets: readonly SceneTargetRequirementV2[];\n  playbackPolicy: ScenePlaybackPolicy;\n  acknowledgmentPolicy: SceneAcknowledgmentPolicy;\n  finalStatePolicy: SceneFinalStateHandoff;\n  reducedFallback: \"semantic-final-state\" | \"static-reader\" | \"none\";\n  timeoutMs: number;\n  replacedBy?: string;\n}>;\n```\n\nThe v1 adapter converts singular string host kinds and local requirements to v2 only inside a registered compatibility host. It may support tests and staged migration but cannot remain the production route at Phase 2 completion.\n\n`AnimationDirector.play()` accepts a registered host controller/handle. Raw `{ root, hostId, hostKind }` is restricted to an explicit legacy/development adapter and rejected for production scenes.\n\nExisting receipt and outcome names remain compatible. New host generation, target resolution, ownership, handoff, and cleanup evidence extends receipts without weakening acknowledgment rules.\n\nAll 28 scenes receive an explicit Phase 2 disposition. Current baseline totals are 16 production, 4 legacy, 5 future-contract, and 3 deprecated. A `merged` disposition is added for retained traceability when execution has moved into another authoritative state machine. Deprecated and merged records remain in requirement tracking even when executable builders are removed.\n\n### 3.20 Frozen item 20 — Phase 3 and Phase 4 migration\n\nThe architecture below is the only SceneHost/ownership system for later phases. Phase 3 and Phase 4 may add hosts and contracts, not parallel registries or selector conventions.\n\n## 4. Provider-scoped registries\n\n`AnimationProvider` owns one `SceneHostRegistry` and one `AnimationOwnershipRegistry`, created once per provider instance and injected into the Director and React contexts.\n\nModule-global mutable registries are prohibited. Multiple providers and tests remain isolated. Provider teardown releases all active and held instances, runtime surfaces, external handles, ownership claims, observers, timers, and diagnostics.\n\nThe host registry validates duplicate IDs, exact registration tokens, root connectivity, provider membership, nested-host boundaries, and stale generations. Normal React cleanup is immediate; pruning on public operations and a bounded observer provide backstop cleanup for unexpectedly detached roots.\n\nThe ownership registry maintains:\n\n- element → normalized group → active claims;\n- claim ID → sanitized metadata plus weak element reference;\n- scope and host indexes for exact backstop release;\n- active-count snapshots for lifecycle validation.\n\n## 5. Canonical host-local target resolution\n\nEach invocation performs one host scan and constructs one immutable resolved target set. Preflight, ownership, builders, receipts, and cleanup all use that same set.\n\nA local candidate qualifies only when:\n\n1. the registered root is connected and still owns the registration token;\n2. root ID and kind agree with the registry;\n3. the candidate is contained by the root;\n4. its nearest `[data-scene-host-id]` is the active root, excluding nested-host descendants;\n5. its nearest explicit scene-instance boundary is the active immutable instance;\n6. its declared part and source match the contract;\n7. it is not a hidden PageFlip source, stale clone, temporary curl clone, or disallowed off-page target;\n8. it is connected, sufficiently visible, nontransparent as required, and has a qualifying box;\n9. cardinality is satisfied by current qualifying candidates only;\n10. its required property claims are atomically granted;\n11. its exact handle is cached for the builder.\n\nRejected source, stale, hidden, detached, foreign-host, and off-page candidates remain visible in diagnostics but do not count toward cardinality and never enter the builder set.\n\nRequired claim failure rejects the scene without partial leases. Optional conflict excludes that optional target; it cannot be recovered by a raw query.\n\n`SceneBuildContext` exposes the immutable handle set and owned runtime adapters. It does not expose a general root-query escape hatch. Existing `parts(root, ...)` and direct scene selectors are removed from production builders. Root animation itself requires a declared root target and permit.\n\n## 6. Permit-gated runtime integration\n\nA granted `AnimationWritePermit` is the only path to an animated write. It revalidates claim, scope, host generation, scene instance, target connection, and requested property group at each mutation boundary.\n\nGSAP adapters map tween/plugin keys to ownership groups, including transforms, `autoAlpha`, DrawSVG, MotionPath, FLIP, CSS variables, and other installed plugins. Undeclared properties fail with structured runtime evidence.\n\nMotion uses owned wrappers that initially render without `layout`, `layoutId`, `animate`, or presence writes, acquire through a synchronous ref boundary, and enable only after grant. A normal effect is too late to prove that Motion did not already write.\n\nRive, Lottie, and PageFlip initialize only after their runtime-surface lease is granted and destroy before release. dnd-kit claims transform synchronously at drag start and releases after end, cancel, abort, failure, or unmount. CSS claims only its active animation/transition interval.\n\nStatic or lint checks should reject direct production scene queries, direct unowned GSAP tween calls, and ungated third-party runtime initialization outside approved adapters.\n\n## 7. Required high-risk wrapper migrations\n\n### Voyage Chart\n\n```text\nMotion-owned semantic marker button\n  aria-hidden GSAP-owned marker visual\n```\n\nThe outer button retains focus, accessible naming, native activation, layout, and interaction feedback. The inner child alone owns stamp/pulse/reveal properties. Explicit location and route keys select targets; array index and DOM order are prohibited. Permanent Chart content uses a section-enhancement host; temporary reveal overlays use a separate progression host.\n\n`marker-stamp` and `ship-course` remain future contracts/`architecture_ready`, not implemented visuals. Existing marker-inspection interaction must not be invented in Phase 2.\n\n### Ship’s Log\n\n```text\nMotion-owned semantic motion.li\n  stable readable log content\n  aria-hidden GSAP fresh-ink child\n  aria-hidden GSAP symbol child\n```\n\nThe row never receives GSAP transform or opacity. `PublicLogEntry.key`/progress-event identity selects the new entry; “last row” inference is prohibited. The initial event overlay remains separate from the authoritative row until snapshot/render ordering makes a permanent-row handoff truthful.\n\nThe unified `TallTaleJournalSession` currently lacks a Ship’s Log projection. Phase 2 must not claim that surface migrated unless a separately approved product/API change adds it.\n\n### Artifact Inspection\n\n```text\nMotion dialog/presence wrapper\n  dialog-local SceneHost\n    Motion shared-layout artifact shell\n      aria-hidden GSAP engraving child\n      aria-hidden GSAP detail-light child\n    stable readable semantic story card\n```\n\nNo GSAP FLIP or transform applies to the Motion `layoutId` node. Focus enters and remains in the dialog, then returns to the exact connected trigger after Motion exit.\n\nWhether `artifact-inspection` becomes a production inner-detail scene or remains a future contract is an unresolved disposition; whole-object GSAP is prohibited either way.\n\n### Companion Header and Navigation\n\nSemantic interaction roots remain Motion/React-owned. Separate pointer-inert, `aria-hidden` GSAP overlays use explicit names such as:\n\n- `companion-header-dim`\n- `companion-desktop-navigation-dim`\n- `companion-mobile-navigation-dim`\n\nGeneric `peripheral` selection is retired. Each overlay has deliberate `0..1` cardinality. Chapter scenes receive registered external handles rather than querying the Player root.\n\n### Quartermaster\n\nOrdinary command controls, form fields, dialogs, result rows, and receipts remain Motion/React-owned. Each command animation receives a dedicated `quartermaster-command` host and instance-local GSAP overlay. The Director cannot select controls or unrelated cards.\n\n### Access and login\n\nMotion owns form presence, pending/error/permission state, and validation. GSAP owns only a bounded accepted/rejected cinematic child and route-transition ceremony. Successful navigation holds or commits its accepted pose until route handoff; failure returns a stable interactive state. Exact behavior remains subject to the R6 handoff.\n\nDecorative GSAP children are `aria-hidden` and pointer-inert. Semantic roles, focus, labels, form controls, and readable content remain on their existing interaction nodes.\n\n## 8. Final-state and cleanup invariants\n\nSuccessful route-changing scenes cannot visibly return to closed, locked, or rejected state before navigation. Failed requests cannot flash a success state. Repeated submissions cannot release or overwrite another submission’s held instance.\n\nA route-changing success normally uses `hold-final-until-unmount`. A temporary event overlay that settles into permanent application state uses `reconcile-then-revert`. Pure decoration uses `revert-immediately`. Semantic state that becomes application truth uses `commit-final-state`. Runtime failure may use only the contract’s approved `fallback-to-static-state`.\n\nA handoff transfers the final pose, associated context, and claims as one unit. Cleanup cannot release the ownership claim first and leave an unowned retained transform. A later invocation, semantic commit, route unmount, or host release closes the held instance.\n\n## 9. Compatibility and scene disposition rules\n\nAt completion:\n\n- every production scene uses a registered host and v2 contract;\n- no production builder performs document-wide, application-root, or raw cross-tree target queries;\n- no production scene uses DOM order for semantic identity;\n- no hidden or stale PageFlip node qualifies;\n- fake page-turn builders are non-executable tombstones;\n- `journal-open` does not compete with the authoritative opening state machine;\n- future contracts such as `chapter-heading`, `prose-ink`, `marker-stamp`, `ship-course`, and conditional `artifact-inspection` retain explicit truthful dispositions;\n- current broad Phase 1 host strings are normalized into typed host-kind sets.\n\nThe current single Director timeline may serialize active playback while multiple hosts remain registered. Queueing and priority cannot change a handle’s host or identity.\n\n## 10. Phase 3 handoff\n\nPhase 3, Unfurl the Tale, receives stable APIs for:\n\n- persistent Player progression host;\n- global event host;\n- section-local enhancement hosts;\n- immutable scene/replay identity;\n- host/external target contracts;\n- ownership claims and write permits;\n- final-state handoff;\n- replay-instance identity;\n- privacy-safe presentation diagnostics.\n\nRequired usage patterns are:\n\n- chapter release: persistent progression host plus explicit companion dim handles;\n- map location/route reveal: temporary progression host handing keyed targets to the Chart enhancement host;\n- artifact award: explicit source/destination handles;\n- quest discovery: visible PageFlip primary target only;\n- new log entry: event host followed by keyed semantic reconciliation;\n- finale tease: explicit target handles and held/committed semantic state.\n\nPhase 3 must not reinvent SceneHost, target resolution, ownership, PageFlip filtering, final-state handoff, or instance identity.\n\n## 11. Phase 4 handoff\n\nPhase 4, Bring the Harbor Alive, consumes:\n\n- `platform-ceremony` hosts for modern access and invitation flows;\n- the access/login hold-and-route handoff policy;\n- Motion semantic outer/GSAP cinematic child wrappers;\n- library-card component ownership scopes;\n- invitation and waiting-room hosts;\n- Studio dnd-kit drag scopes with separate post-drop Motion/GSAP children;\n- the same diagnostics and ownership registry.\n\nPhase 4 does not create an independent platform animation registry or broad selector convention.\n\n## 12. Zero-loss requirement tracking\n\nThe immutable preservation denominator is:\n\n- 220 Codex matrix records;\n- 238 OA records;\n- 458 total source records.\n\nCross-source equivalence is a relationship, not deduplication.\n\nThe current zero-loss gate fails:\n\n- the OA reconciliation ledger is missing;\n- the sole 238-row OA source is untracked;\n- all 220 matrix rows lack the required authoritative roadmap-phase field;\n- all 238 OA rows lack authoritative mapping and phase assignment;\n- current accepted-unmapped count is therefore 458;\n- current fully Phase-2-traceable count is 0.\n\nPhase 2 must:\n\n1. preserve all 220 Codex IDs and all `OA-001`–`OA-238`;\n2. create the 238-row OA reconciliation ledger;\n3. add the ten required tracking columns to the matrix;\n4. preserve separate coverage and implementation-status dimensions;\n5. allow new matrix rows when an OA requirement has no truthful target;\n6. record phase, library, trigger, replay, reduced behavior, acceptance criteria, evidence, blocker, and replacement rationale;\n7. keep rejected, superseded, deprecated, and merged rows permanently traceable;\n8. mark architecture-enabled future visuals `architecture_ready`, never `implemented`;\n9. validate that all 458 source records have a valid mapping and phase;\n10. finish with accepted requirements unmapped equal to zero.\n\nHistorical counts and findings remain frozen baseline evidence. Documentation updates add current status rather than rewriting history.\n\n## 13. Required validation design\n\n### SceneHost and target resolution\n\nCover:\n\n- unique registration and duplicate rejection;\n- unique live/replay instance identity;\n- host-local resolution;\n- identical part names across hosts;\n- stale instance and detached-host rejection;\n- nested-host exclusion;\n- external handle validation;\n- outside-host rejection;\n- unmount during playback;\n- idempotent cleanup;\n- simultaneous registered Player and Quartermaster hosts;\n- showcase isolation;\n- hidden source, visible primary, stale clone, permanent target, temporary overlay, disconnected, zero-box, and transparent fixtures.\n\nThe mandatory release-blocking two-host test mounts two `route-path` parts, plays only Host A, and proves exact A-only mutation, metrics, claims, cleanup, stale-handle rejection, and Host B continued usability.\n\n### Ownership\n\nCover every required pair:\n\n- GSAP/Motion\n- GSAP/CSS\n- Motion/CSS\n- Motion/PageFlip\n- GSAP/PageFlip\n- GSAP/Rive\n- GSAP/Lottie\n- Motion `layoutId`/GSAP transform\n- dnd-kit/Motion\n- dnd-kit/GSAP\n\nAlso cover first grant, same-scope reentrancy, different-scope conflict, no permit on rejection, atomic batch failure, alias normalization, non-overlapping groups, exact/idempotent release, abort/interruption/fallback cleanup, stale sweep, nested wrappers, and baseline restoration.\n\n### PageFlip\n\nCover all fourteen required cases: source exclusion, visible-primary qualification, stale rejection, current-page qualification, off-page exclusion, ID/IDREF integrity, accessibility tree, manual/keyboard/programmatic PageFlip ownership, fake-scene retirement, logical identity across content update and orientation, and complete unmount cleanup.\n\n### Components and final state\n\nCover Chart, Log, Artifact, Companion, Quartermaster, Access, and Login ownership boundaries, explicit identity, focus, keyboard operation, reduced behavior, stable semantic content, and cleanup.\n\nAccess/login covers success, accepted pose, delayed route, no snapback, route failure, authentication failure, abort, unmount, reduced mode, and repeated submission.\n\n### Lifecycle, accessibility, browser, and performance\n\nRun at least twenty cycles of host mount/unmount, play/cleanup, artifact open/close, PageFlip update/unmount, and non-mutating Quartermaster overlay. Host, target, handle, claim, listener, timer, and retained-node counts return to baseline.\n\nValidate the six required viewports:\n\n```text\n2560x1440\n1920x1080\n1440x900\n430x932\n390x844\n844x390\n```\n\nPreserve semantic roles, dialog trapping and focus return, native controls, hidden-source exclusion, readable visible pages, stable focus order, reduced-state readability, and motion-independent state signals.\n\nMutating browser tests remain blocked until R10’s isolated-database proof succeeds. One owner controls the server, ports, copied database, nonce, browser mutation suite, build, E2E, and final `npm run validate`.\n\n## 14. Frozen decisions\n\n- Mounted host and scene invocation are separate identities.\n- Host and ownership registries are provider-scoped, not module-global.\n- Contract v2 uses typed host-kind sets and explicit host/external target sources.\n- The preflight-approved target set is the only builder target set.\n- Nested hosts are legal; parent resolution stops at the nearest nested host boundary.\n- External handles are registry-minted, explicit, scoped, property-limited, and stale-rejecting.\n- Required ownership acquisition is atomic.\n- Write permits, not attributes, enforce ownership.\n- Same runtime in different scopes can conflict.\n- `width` and `height` normalize to one `size` group.\n- Motion `layout` and `layoutId` normalize to `transform`.\n- Different runtimes use separate nodes for overlapping groups.\n- Held final states retain contexts, instances, and claims until handoff or unmount.\n- Hidden sources, temporary clones, stale clones, and off-page content do not count toward PageFlip cardinality.\n- StPageFlip exclusively owns physical page turns.\n- Deprecated scene IDs remain traceable after executable fake behavior is removed.\n- The zero-loss denominator remains 220 Codex plus 238 OA records.\n\n## 15. Principal risks\n\n| Risk | Required control |\n|---|---|\n| Motion writes before an effect-based claim | Gated ref wrapper; initially omit active Motion props; prove with style-write tests |\n| GSAP plugin property escapes normalization | Central adapter with exhaustive plugin/property mapping and fail-closed unknowns |\n| Required target acquisition leaves partial claims | Atomic claim transaction and rollback |\n| Builder bypasses rejected optional target | Remove raw root/selector access; immutable resolved handles only |\n| Held final pose leaks claims | Held-instance registry state, next-invocation replacement, host-release backstop |\n| PageFlip temporary clone duplicates IDs/content | Object-identity tracking, ID/IDREF rewriting, inert temporary copies |\n| Detached roots or targets remain indexed | Weak references, pruning before operations/diagnostics, bounded detach observer |\n| Provider nesting or Strict Mode cross-contaminates state | Injected provider registries and token-specific idempotent cleanup |\n| Requirement consolidation loses accepted work | Immutable 220/238 denominators, explicit reverse mappings, zero-unmapped validator |\n| Phase 1 changes during final tests invalidate assumptions | Re-read final Phase 1 diff/reports before approving this record |\n\n## 16. Unresolved decisions and required handoffs\n\nThese items must be resolved before the coordinator marks the record approved:\n\n1. **Phase 1 handoff:** Revalidate the final Phase 1 working tree after its pending browser fixes, build/full validation, reports, synchronization, and final commit state. Do not claim Phase 1 closed from the 297 focused tests alone.\n\n2. **R6 — Quartermaster and access/login:** Incorporate the exact route-commit signal, success hold lifetime, failure restoration, repeated-submission arbitration, focus policy, and command-overlay target mapping.\n\n3. **R7 — PageFlip implementation detail:** The architecture is frozen, but implementation must prove how StPageFlip temporary clones are marked/sanitized across library updates and must obtain real browser/accessibility evidence for duplicate content and focus.\n\n4. **R8 — scene inventory:** Apply the exact per-scene host-kind set, raw-selector migration list, and final `production`/`legacy`/`future-contract`/`merged`/`deprecated` disposition. Preserve the verified 28-scene denominator unless an explicit tombstone migration documents a change.\n\n5. **R9 — requirement reconciliation:** Produce exact OA-to-matrix mappings and phase assignments. Do not guess mappings merely to reach zero. Resolve the missing ledger, untracked OA source, absent normalized columns, and current 458-unmapped gate.\n\n6. **R10 — validation isolation:** Incorporate the exact isolated-database command sequence, runtime ownership record, nonce proof, canonical before/after evidence, validation ports, and failure classification.\n\n7. **Artifact Inspection:** Decide whether its inner engraving/detail sequence gains a real production trigger after dialog focus or remains a truthful future contract. Whole-object GSAP remains prohibited.\n\n8. **Unified Player runtime:** Chart and Ship’s Log are not present in `TallTaleJournalSession`. Phase 2 must either explicitly scope migration to the compatibility Player surface or obtain separate product/API authority before adding those surfaces.\n\n9. **Marker interaction:** Preserve native focus and activation only. A new inspection/selection behavior belongs to a later requirement unless product intent explicitly moves it into Phase 2.\n\n10. **Concurrent playback:** The design assumes multiple registered hosts with one serialized Director timeline. If product acceptance requires two genuinely concurrent active cinematic timelines, that is a separate Director scheduling decision; host, target, and claim scopes already support it.\n\n## 17. Required-definition checklist\n\n- [x] 1. `SceneHost`\n- [x] 2. `SceneHostKind`\n- [x] 3. `SceneHostRegistration`\n- [x] 4. `SceneHostHandle`\n- [x] 5. `SceneInstanceId`\n- [x] 6. `SceneHostId`\n- [x] 7. `SceneTargetHandle`\n- [x] 8. `ExternalSceneTargetHandle`\n- [x] 9. `OwnershipScope`\n- [x] 10. `AnimationOwnershipClaim`\n- [x] 11. `AnimationOwnershipClaimResult`\n- [x] 12. `AnimatedProperty`\n- [x] 13. `PropertyOwnershipGroup`\n- [x] 14. `SceneFinalStateHandoff`\n- [x] 15. `SceneCleanupPolicy`\n- [x] 16. `PageFlipSourceBoundary`\n- [x] 17. `PageFlipCloneBoundary`\n- [x] 18. Scene-host diagnostics\n- [x] 19. Existing-scene compatibility\n- [x] 20. Phase 3 and Phase 4 migration\n\nExplanatory coverage:\n\n- [x] Application-root querying risk\n- [x] Host versus scene identity\n- [x] Live versus replay identity\n- [x] Duplicate part names across isolated hosts\n- [x] External handles replacing cross-tree selectors\n- [x] Enforced ownership rejection\n- [x] Motion participation\n- [x] Wrapper responsibility separation\n- [x] Hidden PageFlip-source exclusion\n- [x] Final-state handoff and snapback prevention\n- [x] Phase 3 persistent Player progression use\n- [x] Phase 4 platform-ceremony use\n- [x] Future Codex/OA zero-loss tracking\n\n## Confidence and gaps\n\nConfidence is high for the core host, target-resolution, ownership, wrapper, PageFlip, cleanup, compatibility, diagnostics, and zero-loss architecture because the current implementation and completed R2/R3/R4/R5/R7/R9 evidence converge on the same failure modes.\n\nConfidence is medium for exact access/login, Quartermaster, final per-scene dispositions, browser isolation commands, and PageFlip assistive-technology behavior pending R6, R8, R10, browser evidence, and the final Phase 1 handoff.\n\nNo source files, Git state, runtime, tests, database, package files, or documentation were modified by this draft lane."}},{"agent_name":"/root/r4_chart_log","agent_status":{"completed":"R4 complete — both read-only child lanes finished, were reviewed, and reconciled. No files, runtime, tests, database, or Git state changed.\n\n### Verified production surfaces\n\n| Surface | Component | Production scenes | Current target conflict |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Voyage Chart | `src/components/player/workspace/VoyageChart.tsx` | `map-reveal`, `route-draw` | `motion.button` also carries GSAP target; CSS writes the same transform |\n| Ship’s Log | `src/components/player/workspace/ShipsLog.tsx` | `log-entry` | `motion.li` also carries GSAP transform/opacity target |\n\n`PlayerExperience.tsx` maps:\n\n- `MAP_LOCATION_REVEALED` → `map-reveal`\n- `MAP_ROUTE_REVEALED` → `route-draw`\n- `JOURNAL_ANNOTATION_ADDED` and `PLAYER_LOG_ENTRY_ADDED` → `log-entry`\n\nIt currently plays these against the entire Player `<main>` while `PlayerProgressionProp` mounts temporary targets sharing the permanent components’ part names.\n\n### Voyage Chart migration\n\nConfirmed issues:\n\n- Marker `motion.button` combines Motion `layout`, GSAP `map-marker-new`, and CSS `transform: translate(-50%, -50%)`.\n- Marker identity uses array position, including a filtered-index/unfiltered-length mismatch.\n- Every route exposes generic `route-path`; multiple routes violate exact-one selection.\n- The temporary event overlay duplicates marker, route, and fog targets when Chart is mounted.\n- Brief-assumed press/click behavior is not actually implemented: current marker buttons have no click handler or `whileTap`.\n- Reduced `map-reveal` still begins with scale/rotation; shortened duration is not a nonspatial reduced state.\n\nRequired boundary:\n\n```text\nMotion-owned semantic marker button\n  GSAP-owned decorative marker visual\n    stable marker content\n```\n\nUse explicit location/route keys or external target handles, never index or DOM order. Remove CSS transform from the Motion node. The permanent chart should use a `player-section-enhancement` host; the event overlay should use a separate `player-progression` host.\n\nKeep `marker-stamp` and `ship-course` truthful as future-contract/architecture-ready scenes, not implemented production animations.\n\n### Ship’s Log migration\n\nConfirmed issues:\n\n- `motion.li` directly owns `log-entry-new`, so Motion layout and GSAP transform collide.\n- “New” is selected as the last entry inside every day group; multi-day logs therefore create multiple required targets.\n- The temporary overlay adds another `log-entry-new`.\n- The authoritative new row does not exist until the post-scene snapshot refresh. Current production playback animates the temporary overlay, not the committed row.\n- `.player-event-prop` applies CSS centering transform to the same outer log overlay GSAP moves.\n- Reachability metadata names only `PLAYER_LOG_ENTRY_ADDED`, although annotations also invoke `log-entry`.\n\nRequired boundary:\n\n```text\nMotion-owned motion.li row\n  stable semantic log content\n  aria-hidden GSAP fresh-ink child: log-entry-new\n  aria-hidden GSAP symbol child: log-symbol-new\n```\n\n`PublicLogEntry.key` equals the progress event ID, providing an explicit identity. Stable text, `<time>`, and route-back buttons must remain outside GSAP opacity/transform children. The authoritative row cannot become the initial required scene target until refresh/render ordering changes.\n\n### Exact focused tests\n\nPrefer intentional new files to avoid ownership conflicts:\n\n- `src/components/player/workspace/VoyageChart.test.tsx`\n\n  - Motion outer versus GSAP inner are distinct nodes.\n  - Insertion, update, and reordering preserve keyed identity and focus.\n  - Only an explicit location key receives `map-marker-new`.\n  - Only an explicit route key receives `route-path`.\n  - Marker focus/native activation and decorative accessibility are preserved.\n  - Reduced state is nonspatial and readable.\n\n- `src/components/player/workspace/ShipsLog.test.tsx`\n\n  - Multi-day data still yields exactly one explicit ink target.\n  - No `motion.li` has a GSAP scene part or GSAP transform/opacity claim.\n  - Ink and symbol children are separate, decorative GSAP targets.\n  - Filtering preserves select focus, correct entries, and navigation actions.\n  - Insertion preserves existing row identity and does not steal focus.\n  - Reduced and empty-filter states remain semantic/readable.\n\nShared integration coverage:\n\n- Player event host cannot resolve permanent Chart/Log section targets.\n- Mandatory two-host `map-reveal`/`route-draw` `route-path` isolation fixture.\n- Same-node Motion/GSAP claims reject; nested-wrapper claims succeed.\n- Success, failure, skip, interruption, and unmount return claims/hosts to baseline.\n- Twenty lifecycle cycles.\n- All six required viewports for overflow, stacking, focus order, controls, and reduced readability.\n\n### Shared dependencies and ownership\n\nComponent writers must wait for the shared APIs to freeze:\n\n- SceneHost registry and immutable scene instances\n- host-local resolved target delivery\n- external key-based handles\n- structural Motion ownership claims\n- property-group enforcement and cleanup\n- final-state/handoff policy\n\nRecommended write ownership after freeze:\n\n- Component lane: `VoyageChart.tsx`, `ShipsLog.tsx`, and the two new dedicated tests\n- Shared integration owner only: `PlayerExperience.tsx`, `story.scene.ts`, `scene-registry.ts`\n- One style owner: `src/styles/player.css`\n\n`TallTaleJournalSession` contains neither Voyage Chart nor Ship’s Log integration. Ship’s Log exists only on compatibility `/tale/[campaignSlug]`; the unified Tall Tale journal routes cannot truthfully be reported as migrated without a new authoritative projection/API and product-placement decision.\n\nThe required `Development_Docs/Animation_Original_Audit_Reconciliation_Ledger.csv` is also absent, so OA zero-loss reconciliation cannot yet be proven.\n\nConfidence is high for the ownership, duplicate-target, identity, cardinality, lifecycle, and test-gap findings; medium for final host API names and marker activation behavior pending shared-contract freeze and product intent."}},{"agent_name":"/root/w1_phase1_bounded_paint","agent_status":{"completed":"W1 is stopped and read-only.\n\n- Initial hashes matched, so there was no ownership conflict.\n- Added a 100 ms watchdog to `nextPaint`, with timeout cleanup on paint or abort.\n- Added two regressions for suspended rAF progression and abort cleanup.\n- Focused Vitest run passed: 1 file, 14/14 tests, exit 0.\n- The test run preceded only the final behavior-neutral extraction of `100` into `paintAcquisitionTimeoutMs`; that last refactor was not rerun.\n- Read-only inspection confirms the patch is syntactically complete.\n- No files outside the two assigned paths were edited; no Git or runtime state was changed."}}]}

This tool takes no arguments.\\n\\nexec tool declaration:\\n```ts\\ndeclare const tools: { codex_app__read_thread_terminal(args: {}): Promise<unknown>; };\\n```\"},{\"name\":\"codex_app__wait_threads\",\"description\":\"Tools provided by the Codex app.\\n\\nWait for the first of up to eight Codex threads to complete or need attention. Use timeoutMs: 0 for an immediate snapshot. Commentary never wakes the wait. An up-to-date cursor omits previously delivered final text; a timeout includes compact progress for all targets. Per-target failures are returned in errors.\\n\\nexec tool declaration:\\n```ts\\ndeclare const tools: { codex_app__wait_threads(args: {\\n  // Threads to wait for. The first target that completes or needs attention wins.\\n  targets: Array<{\\n  // Optional cursor returned by an earlier wait.\\n  afterCursor?: string;\\n  // Optional host id returned by create_thread or list_threads.\\n  hostId?: string;\\n  // Thread id to wait for.\\n  threadId: string;\\n}>;\\n  // Maximum event-wait time in milliseconds. ## Assistant
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T22:09:39.148Z

Phase 1 has now integrated all three known fixes: Quartermaster’s per-action host contract, replay’s true database-baseline assertion, and cleanup timing from actual Journal disconnection. It is repairing one validation-mirror asset and then rerunning the complete gate. On the Phase 2 side, PageFlip planning confirmed a crucial library behavior: portrait turns create undocumented temporary clones that copy IDs and scene markers, so the implementation must combine generation revocation with runtime clone sanitation—not rely on attributes alone.

## Assistant
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T22:11:30.983Z

Preparation wave complete and reviewed; all three slots are released.

- P1 produced the exact dependency-ordered core API/file/test blueprint and confirmed every architecture hotspot overlaps Phase 1, so starting a writer early would risk overwriting its work.
- P2 proved the PageFlip clone lifecycle and defined synchronous temporary-clone sanitation, generation revocation, ID/IDREF rewriting, logical-page preservation, and 20 focused cases.
- P3 froze access/command handoffs: claims remain held through route or semantic commit, repeated submissions are first-wins, Quartermaster uses an instance-local decorative host, and StaffSignIn remains a Phase 4 visual consumer.

No files or shared resources changed. The write wave can start immediately once Phase 1 commits and releases the checkout.

Decision summary\n30:- preserve the exact 28-scene reachability disposition: 16 production, 4 legacy, 5 future contracts, and 3 deprecated;\n32:- stop after Phase 2 validation and synchronization; Phase 3 does not begin automatically.\n36:## 2. Why the current compatibility boundary is insufficient\n44:## 3. Sources, dependency gate, and pending baseline procedure\n62:## 4. Authority and lifetime model\n91:## 5. Frozen identities and `SceneHostKind`\n118:## 6. `SceneHost`, registration, and host handle\n155:## 7. Immutable scene invocation\n190:## 8. Version 2 target contracts\n228:A Phase 1 v1 adapter may translate one verified legacy host kind and split required/optional arrays into `targets`, but it cannot broaden an unknown kind, infer an external handle, weaken cardinality, or make deprecated work production-reachable. All 16 production scenes must use native v2 contracts before the compatibility adapter can be disabled for production playback.\n230:## 9. Host-local target registration and resolution\n284:## 10. Explicit external target handles\n307:## 11. Runtime owners, properties, and ownership groups\n364:## 12. Claims, atomicity, and write permits\n413:## 13. Motion, CSS, PageFlip, and drag integration\n435:## 14. Final-state handoff and cleanup\n510:## 15. PageFlip source and clone boundary\n547:## 16. Exact scene disposition\n560:## 17. High-risk component boundaries\n562:### 17.1 Voyage Chart\n566:### 17.2 Ship's Log\n570:### 17.3 Artifact Inspection and Treasure Altar\n574:### 17.4 Companion Header and Navigation\n578:### 17.5 Quartermaster\n582:### 17.6 Access and login\n584:Motion owns form presence, pending state, validation errors, and permission state. GSAP owns a bounded accepted/rejected cinematic child and route-transition ceremony. Future Rive lock/seal state remains internal to its wrapper. Success holds until navigation; route failure restores stable focusable UI; auth failure never flashes success. Phase 2 migrates the current legacy `AccessGate` and Quartermaster access boundary. Modern Player/Staff platform visuals remain Phase 4 work.\n586:## 18. Diagnostics, fallback, and privacy\n610:## 19. Compatibility and migration sequence\n625:The temporary v1 adapter is one-way and observable. It records compatibility use, rejects document-wide queries and unknown host kinds, and is not available to new Phase 2 components. It is deleted or limited to the four named legacy scenes after all 16 production scenes migrate. No lane may create a parallel host or ownership API.\n627:## 20. Phase 3 handoff: Unfurl the Tale\n629:Phase 3 consumes these stable APIs:\n640:Chapter release, map location reveal, route reveal, artifact award, quest discovery, log insertion, and finale tease each mount or reuse the appropriate host, register semantic targets by stable domain identity, pass external handles where continuity crosses trees, and use a fresh invocation for replay. Phase 3 must not reinvent host, resolver, ownership, PageFlip filtering, handoff, or identity architecture. This record does not authorize implementation of the complete persistent Player progression UI during Phase 2.\n642:## 21. Phase 4 handoff: Bring the Harbor Alive\n644:Phase 4 consumes `platform-ceremony`, `gateway`, and `access` hosts; access/login handoff; Motion/GSAP wrapper ownership; explicit library-card targets; invitation and waiting-room host patterns; and dnd-kit ownership during Studio drag.\n646:Library cards keep Motion layout on the outer node and cinematic children inside. Invitation and waiting-room ceremonies are local hosts with safe target contracts. Studio acquires the `spatial-transform` group for dnd-kit only during drag and does not permit Motion or GSAP transforms on that same node. Modern PlayerSignIn and StaffSignIn visuals are Phase 4, not legacy AccessGate scope. Phase 4 may extend scenes but cannot create an independent ownership registry.\n650:## 22. Zero-loss requirement model\n657:Accepted total:           458\n708:## 23. Validation ownership and required evidence\n714:### 23.1 SceneHost and two-host release gate\n720:### 23.2 Ownership\n724:### 23.3 Components and final state\n728:### 23.4 PageFlip and accessibility\n732:### 23.5 Lifecycle and viewport\n749:### 23.6 Integrated commands and classification\n765:## 24. Shared resource and file-ownership rules\n771:## 25. Frozen decisions\n786:| D12 | Scene disposition is exactly 16 production, 4 legacy, 5 future-contract, 3 deprecated. | Matches current source-grounded reachability and preserves traceability. |\n787:| D13 | Accepted denominator is 458; OA mapping is 97 existing plus 141 dedicated rows. | Prevents broad Codex rows from erasing specific OA requirements. |\n791:## 26. Risk register\n796:| R2 | A compatibility adapter preserves application-root behavior. | High | Instrument adapter use; forbid it for new components; migrate all 16 production scenes. |\n808:## 27. Completion gate for this design\n810:The architecture in this record is frozen for implementation after the Phase 1 procedure in section 3 confirms compatibility. Component writers may begin only after shared source interfaces and focused host/ownership tests are green. Any change to identity, host authority, target source, property normalization, permit enforcement, final-state ordering, PageFlip generation, or the 458-requirement model requires a recorded decision update and coordinator review.\n814:After those gates pass, Phase 2 stops for review. [{"text": "Script completed\nWall time 0.4 seconds\nOutput:\n", "type": "input_text"}, {"text": "Exit code: 0\nWall time: 0.4 seconds\nOutput:\n\r\n## 21. Phase 4 handoff: Bring the Harbor Alive\r\n\r\nPhase 4 consumes `platform-ceremony`, `gateway`, and `access` hosts; access/login handoff; Motion/GSAP wrapper ownership; explicit library-card targets; invitation and waiting-room host patterns; and dnd-kit ownership during Studio drag.\r\n\r\nLibrary cards keep Motion layout on the outer node and cinematic children inside. Invitation and waiting-room ceremonies are local hosts with safe target contracts. Studio acquires the `spatial-transform` group for dnd-kit only during drag and does not permit Motion or GSAP transforms on that same node. Modern PlayerSignIn and StaffSignIn visuals are Phase 4, not legacy AccessGate scope. Phase 4 may extend scenes but cannot create an independent ownership registry.\r\n\r\nPhases 5 and 6 are referenced only as `Phase 5 (name not specified in governing brief)` and `Phase 6 (name not specified in governing brief)` until a governing document names them.\r\n\r\n## 22. Zero-loss requirement model\r\n\r\nThe accepted source denominator is fixed for this phase:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nCodex audit requirements: 220\r\nOA requirements:          238\r\nAccepted total:           458\r\n```\r\n\r\nPhysical matrix rows and accepted source requirements are separate counts. All 238 OA IDs remain first-class ledger records. Reconciliation maps 97 OA requirements to existing Codex matrix rows and creates 141 dedicated specificity-preserving rows where a broad row would hide the accepted behavior. Thus `97 + 141 = 238`; no consolidation deletes an OA identity and no mirrored matrix row is double-counted as a new accepted source requirement.\r\n\r\nBoth controlling CSVs add or normalize these exact columns:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nImplementation Status\r\nRoadmap Phase\r\nProject Lanternwake Phase\r\nArchitecture Dependency\r\nScene Host Required\r\nOwnership Contract Required\r\nTarget Contract Required\r\nBlocked By\r\nImplemented In Commit\r\nValidation Status\r\n```\r\n\r\nAllowed implementation statuses are:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nnot_started\r\narchitecture_blocked\r\narchitecture_ready\r\npartially_implemented\r\nimplemented\r\nvalidated\r\nblocked\r\nsuperseded\r\nrejected\r\n```\r\n\r\nEach accepted row also retains or gains explicit correct library, trigger, replay policy, reduced-motion behavior, acceptance criteria, source identity, and evidence. `architecture_ready` means the Phase 2 boundary exists; it never means the later visual, trigger, reduced behavior, or acceptance tests are implemented. Historical status/evidence fields remain intact; normalized current fields add current truth without rewriting history.\r\n\r\nThe validator must fail unless:\r\n\r\n1. all 220 Codex IDs are unique and assigned;\r\n2. all `OA-001` through `OA-238` are unique, contiguous, accepted, and assigned;\r\n3. every OA row maps to an existing row or one dedicated row, with totals exactly 97 and 141;\r\n4. every mapping target exists and every accepted row has one roadmap and Project Lanternwake phase;\r\n5. library, trigger, replay, reduced behavior, acceptance criteria, status, and validation status are nonblank and use allowed values;\r\n6. every Phase 2 row has implementation evidence, validation evidence, or an explicit blocker;\r\n7. `implemented` and `validated` have a real implementation path/commit and appropriate test evidence;\r\n8. superseded/rejected rows retain approved rationale and replacement where applicable;\r\n9. the 10 normalized columns exist in both artifacts with exact names;\r\n10. accepted requirements unmapped equals zero.\r\n\r\nThe Codex matrix validator accepts all existing prefixes, including `MX`, `AS`, `AC`, `AM`, `AP`, `AD`, `AR`, `AL`, `AG`, `AA`, and `AK`; it must not drop valid `AA`, `AK`, or `AP` rows through an incomplete prefix allowlist. Phase names must come from governing records. Phase 5 or 6 names are not invented.\r\n\r\n## 23. Validation ownership and required evidence\r\n\r\nOne coordinator-owned validation lane exclusively controls the runtime, browser, copied database, ports, dependency state, production build, E2E, and full `npm run validate`. Component lanes run only focused non-conflicting checks.\r\n\r\nBefore any mutating browser test, the owner creates a unique database copy, supplies an absolute `DATABASE_URL`, records server PID/port, stores and queries an isolation nonce through the running app, records canonical path/hash/size/mtime, and proves the canonical database is unchanged afterward. An alternate worktree or port alone is not isolation. Failure stops mutation tests and is classified `database-isolation` or `environment`, never passed by assumption.\r\n\r\n### 23.1 SceneHost and two-host release gate\r\n\r\nUnit tests cover unique registration, duplicate rejection, unique invocation identity, host-local resolution, identical names across hosts, stale invocation, detached cleanup, nested hosts, external handles, outside-host rejection, unmount, idempotent cleanup, simultaneous Player/Quartermaster hosts, and showcase isolation.\r\n\r\nThe release-blocking integration fixture mounts Host A `map-reveal` / `route-path` and Host B `route-draw` / `route-path`, runs one scene, and proves only that host changes; metrics name the correct instance; cleanup does not affect the other host; target counts are exact; and no claim leaks.\r\n\r\n### 23.2 Ownership\r\n\r\nTests cover GSAP vs Motion, GSAP vs CSS, Motion vs CSS, Motion vs PageFlip, GSAP vs PageFlip, GSAP vs Rive/Lottie containers, Motion `layoutId` vs GSAP transform, dnd-kit vs Motion transform, and dnd-kit vs GSAP transform. They prove first grant, same-runtime compatibility, atomic conflict rejection, rejected-write prevention, release/reclaim, cleanup, normalized property conflicts, non-overlap, stale sweep, interruption, fallback, and nested wrappers.\r\n\r\n### 23.3 Components and final state\r\n\r\nFocused tests prove the ownership boundaries in section 17. Access/login covers success response, accepted pose, delayed route, no snapback, route failure, auth failure, abort, unmount, reduced mode, and repeated submission. Failure never flashes success; success never returns to closed/locked before navigation; focus and controls remain correct.\r\n\r\n### 23.4 PageFlip and accessibility\r\n\r\nTests cover hidden-source exclusion, visible-current clone qualification, stale/off-page rejection, unique IDs, accessibility tree, manual/keyboard/programmatic StPageFlip ownership, deprecated fake curls, update/orientation identity, and unmount release. Semantic roles remain on semantic nodes, decorative children are aria-hidden, visible content remains readable, focus order and dialog trap/return stay correct, route focus waits for navigation, fallbacks are readable, and motion is not the only state signal.\r\n\r\n### 23.5 Lifecycle and viewport\r\n\r\nRun at least 20 cycles each of host mount/unmount, scene play/cleanup, artifact inspection open/close, PageFlip mount/update/unmount, and a non-mutating Quartermaster overlay. After every group, host/target/claim counts return to baseline; stale clones and DOM references are absent; replay uses a new instance; interruptions/routes release claims; and listener/timer counts do not grow monotonically.\r\n\r\nValidate migrated surfaces at:\r\n\r\n```text\r\n2560x1440\r\n1920x1080\r\n1440x900\r\n430x932\r\n390x844\r\n844x390\r\n```\r\n\r\nAssert no horizontal overflow, clipping, wrapper layout drift, focus-order drift, PageFlip geometry error, stacking-context regression, unreachable dialog, or unreadable reduced state.\r\n\r\n### 23.6 Integrated commands and classification\r\n\r\nThe validation report records command, exit code, passed, failed, skipped, blocked, environment errors, baseline failures, and task regressions for format, lint, typecheck, unit/component, SceneHost, ownership, PageFlip, accessibility, browser, viewport, lifecycle, assets, build, E2E, and one full repository validation. Supported top-level gates include:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nnpm run format:check\r\nnpm run lint\r\nnpm run typecheck\r\nnpm test\r\nnpm run assets:validate\r\nnpm run build\r\nnpm run validate\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

I’m bypassing npm’s extra process layer and running one representative Vitest file with a single worker; this will distinguish “Node cannot launch” from “full parallel test pressure” in under two minutes.\",\"phase\":\"commentary\"},{\"type\":\"reasoning\",\"id\":\"item-164\",\"summary\":[\"**Identifying BF6 as performance blocker**\",\"**Evaluating process priority and affinity for tests**\",\"**Identifying BF6 as performance blocker**\",\"**Evaluating process priority and affinity for tests**\",\"**Considering high-priority validation to bypass BF6 impact**\",\"**Identifying BF6 as performance blocker**\",\"**Evaluating process priority and affinity for tests**\",\"**Considering high-priority validation to bypass BF6 impact**\"]},{\"type\":\"agentMessage\",\"id\":\"item-165\",\"text\":\"The single-worker diagnostic finally produced the real blocker: the Phase 1 preflight budget test ran at **225.23 ms p95** against the required **<50 ms**, while the same measured test previously recorded about **9.29 ms** before BF6 was running.
